coredata.dc:title: Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of upconversion composite film and its application on mercury sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2017 The Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: XU, Sai
ZHANG, Yanqiu
LI, Xiangping
ZHANG, Jinsu
SUN, Jiashi
CHENG, Lihong
CHEN, Baojiu
coredata.dc:description: To achieve a stable, sensitive, and high-efficiency biological probe, a novel NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystals/TiO2 inverse opal composite film was designed by self-assembly and solvent evaporation methods. 32-fold enhanced upconversion (UC) emission was investigated under 980 nm excitation. According to size-dependency, excitation power density-dependency as well as photonic stop band (PSB)-dependency upconversion spectra, the enhancement mechanism of the composite film was put down to the stochastical diffraction of IOPCs multi-layered structure to the excitation laser. On the basis of the enhancement effect of the composite film, energy transfer between upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and quantum dots (QDs), and the sensitive sensing of CdTe QDs on mercury, the UC composite film was used for sensing of Hg2+ in serum. The solid sensor as a mercury detector owns lots of superiorities such as feasible operation, good linear relationship (R=0.997), low limit of detection (70.5 nmol/L) and thus may have broad prospects in the biosensing field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: upconversion enhancement
composite film
solid biosensor
mercury detection
rare earths

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: JOURNAL OF RARE EARTHS, Vol. 35, No. 5, May 2017, P. 460 Foundation item: Project supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (11374044), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Uni- versities (3132016333, 3132017058), Doctor Start-up Funds of Liaoning Province (201601067), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M591420) * Corresponding author: CHEN Baojiu (E-mail: bjchen@dlmu.edu.cn; Tel.: +86-411-8472-8909) DOI: 10.1016/S1002-0721(17)60934-7 Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of upconversion composite film and its application on mercury sensing XU Sai (å¾� èµ›)1,2, ZHANG Yanqiu (å¼ è‰³ç§‹)1, LI Xiangping (æ�Žé¦™è��)1, ZHANG Jinsu (å¼ é‡‘è‹�)1, SUN Jiashi (å­™ä½³çŸ³)1, CHENG Lihong (ç¨‹ä¸½çº¢)1, CHEN Baojiu (é™ˆå®�çŽ–)1,* (1. Department of Physics, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, China; 2. Transportation Equipment & Ocean Engineering College, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, China) Received 23 October 2016; revised 6 December 2016 Abstract: To achieve a stable, sensitive, and high-efficiency biological probe, a novel NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystals/TiO2 inverse opal composite film was designed by self-assembly and solvent evaporation methods. 32-fold enhanced upconversion (UC) emission was investigated under 980 nm excitation. According to size-dependency, excitation power density-dependency as well as photonic stop band (PSB)-dependency upconversion spectra, the enhancement mechanism of the composite film was put down to the stochastical diffraction of IOPCs multi-layered structure to the excitation laser. On the basis of the enhancement effect of the composite film, en- ergy transfer between upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and quantum dots (QDs), and the sensitive sensing of CdTe QDs on mercury, the UC composite film was used for sensing of Hg2+ in serum. The solid sensor as a mercury detector owns lots of superior- ities such as feasible operation, good linear relationship (R=0.997), low limit of detection (70.5 nmol/L) and thus may have broad prospects in the biosensing field. Keywords: upconversion enhancement; composite film; solid biosensor; mercury detection; rare earths Upconversion nanoparticles have gained considerable attentions because of their current and future application values in the fields of biological imaging, bio-sensing, infrared photo-dynamical therapeutics, three-dimensional display, fingerprint identification, solar cells, temperature thermometry and so on[1â€“9]. With the further development of life science, rare-earth (RE) doped UCNPs in the field of biosensing have aroused great interests. UCNPs have been successfully used as sensors to detect deoxyribonu- cleic acid (DNA), anions, and molecules[10â€“13]. Compared with traditional luminescence materials commonly used in the biosensing field such as the organic dyes, QDs, lanthanide complex, UCNPs have many unique advan- tages such as sharp emission lines, high signal to noise ratio, and large penetration depth into biological tissues[14]. Unfortunately, for practical applications, due to small absorption cross sections of UCNPs, they generally pos- sess low excitation efficiency. Meanwhile, the structural defects and dipole forbidden transitions also limit their emission efficiency[15]. For example, the bulk NaYF4: Yb3+,Er3+ is usually considered as one of the most effi- cient UC materials; its UC efficiency is only about 3% under 980 nm excitation, however, the UCL efficiency of NaLuF4:Tm3+,Yb3+ UCNPs with sizes ranging from 10 to 20 nm is lower than 0.4%[16]. So improving the UC lu- minescent efficiency of NPs is very important to realize their practical applications on biosensing. Until now, re- searchers have expended great efforts to enhance the lu- minescent efficiency of UCNPs, for instance, looking for more efficient matrix, coating UCNPs with inert shells, and using surface plasmon effect[17â€“23]. In recent years, UC emission enhancement induced by photonic crystals (PCs) effect has been reported by many researchers. PCs are materials with a periodic optical nanostructure which can control the spread of electromagnetic waves[24]. They have attracted extensive interests since the concept was first proposed by Yablonovitch in 1987 due to their ap- plications on photonic device[25]. PCs can be used to modulate the radiative rate, direction, emission wave- length, and have also been utilized to improve the UC luminescence intensity. In the year of 2013, Yin et al. designed a novel NaYF4:Yb,Tm UCNPs/PMMA PCs composite film, which can enhance the UC luminescence as high as 32-fold. They attributed the enhancement ef- fect of UC luminescence to the effective coupling of UCNPs with PMMA PCs[26]. Besides, Yan and his co-workers obtained an enhancement of UC lumines- cence by the improvement of the energy transfer (ET) in XU Sai et al., Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of upconversion composite film and its application on â€¦ 461 inverse opal photonic crystals (IOPCs)[27]. So, the effect of PCs modulation on UC luminescence of NPs is ex- pected to be applied on the future device of biosensor. Inspired by the above ideas, in this work, we intro- duced an efficient strategy to improve emission intensity of NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ UCNPs based on the combining of NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ UCNPs with three-dimensional TiO2 IOPCs. Moreover, benefited by this unique structure and the superior biological compatibility of nanocrystalline TiO2, the composite film can also be developed as a sen- sitive UC biosensor for mercury detection in serum, which was expected to be a stable, feasible and applica- ble UC sensor. 1 Experimental 1.1 Synthesis of NaYF4:Yb,Er Hydrophobic UCNPs were synthesized on the basis of a conventional solvothermal method[28]. Different sizes of NaYF4 NPs were obtained by controlling the reaction temperature at 290, 300, and 320 ÂºC. The preparative nanoparticles were centrifuged with cyclohexane/ethanol mixture thrice, then the final outcomes were preserved in cyclohexane solution for future use. 1.2 Preparation of TiO2 IOPCs The TiO2 IOPCs were synthesized using self-assembled and template removal methods. First, different sizes of monodispersed polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) latex spheres were prepared by controlling the reaction time. Then a piece of glass sheet was vertically placed into a beaker containing 5% solid content colloid suspension of PMMA spheres and put into a 34 ÂºC stove for 20 h. The PMMA spheres self-assembled on the glass substrate into highly ordered arrays slowly. Then the opal temples were put into a 120 ÂºC stove for 40 min to enhance their mechanical strength. Next, 10 mL butyltitanate, 10 mL absolute ethanol, and 1 mL nitric acid were stirred mix- ing for 1 h to form a transparent solution for the prepara- tion of TiO2 precursor sol. Then the precursor solutions were dropped into the interspaces of the PMMA template through capillary force. After infiltration, the resulting products were annealed at the temperature of 500 ÂºC for 3 h. The PSB of IOPCs was well controlled by tuning the sizes of PMMA spheres in the former experimental sec- tion. Moreover, the reference sample (TiO2 powder) was obtained by grinding the sample of the IOPCs to destroy the 3D ordered structure. 1.3 Preparation of NaYF4:Yb,Er UCNPs and TiO2 IOPCs composite films For preparation of the composite film, the glass sub- strate with partial TiO2 IOPCs was placed vertically into the 0.5 mg/mL UCNPs/cyclohexane mixture and placed in a 25 ÂºC oven for 2 h. Actuated by the capillary force, the solvent evaporated slowly and the UCNPs were self-assembled into the interspaces of TiO2 IOPCs or deposited on the glass surface directly. 1.4 Modification of hydrophobic UCNPs with PEI The procedure of modifying OA-NaYF4:Yb,Er with poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) was as follows: 30 mL di- ethylene glycol/PEI mixture solution was heated to 110 ÂºC under argon. The cyclohexane solution contain- ing 1 wt.% UCNPs was slowly injected into the solution and then refluxed at 150 ÂºC for 2.5 h. Subsequently, the cyclohexane was evaporated by heating the solution to 240 ÂºC for 30 min. The as-prepared UCNPs were col- lected via centrifugation with alcohol and deionized wa- ter thrice. 1.5 Synthesis of CdTe QDs CdTe QDs were prepared following the previous lit- erature[29]. In simple terms, CdCl2Â·2.5H2O (0.04 mol/L, 16 mL) was diluted in 200 mL deionized water in a flask, and sodium citrate (400 mg), Na2TeO3 (0.01 mol/L, 4 mL), mercaptosuccinic acid (200 mg), and NaBH4 (400 mg) were added into the flask under a stirring. Then the solu- tion was refluxed under open air for 6 h after the solution color changed to light green. The as-prepared QDs were separated by centrifugation and washed with ethanol thrice. The final products were dissolved in water for the following experiment. 1.6 Detection of Hg2+ with QDs/UCNPs/IOPCs composite films The QDs/UCNPs/IOPCs composite films were pre- pared as a biosensor for sensitive detection of Hg2+, the preparation process was as follows: a certain amount of CdTe QDs and 6 nm PEI coated UCNPs were mixed in the buffer, after being stirred for 30 min at room tem- perature, the TiO2 IOPCs was vertically placed into the mixed solution and placed in a 25 ÂºC oven for over 4 h. Mercuric chloride was used for the Hg2+-sensitivity studies. 20 Î¼L serum solutions containing different con- centrations of Hg2+ were dropped on the composite films, after incubation for 10 min at room temperature, the lu- minescence spectra of the composite film were recorded under 980-nm laser excitation. 2 Results and discussion 2.1 Morphology and structure of TiO2 IOPCs/ NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ composite film Different sizes of NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs were synthe- sized by a typical solvothermal method, and the TiO2 IOPCs were fabricated by template removal method. The NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs were assembled layer-by-layer on 462 JOURNAL OF RARE EARTHS, Vol. 35, No. 5, May 2017 the TiO2 film and glass substrate by solvent evaporation method. The formation process of composite film is shown in Scheme 1(a). Fig. 1(aâ€“c) show the TEM im- ages of the different sizes of NPs synthesized under dif- ferent temperatures (290, 300, 320 ÂºC). It can be ob- served that all of the three NP samples are homogeneous in size and well dispersed. Fig. 1(dâ€“f) show the size dis- tribution histogram of the samples; it can be seen that they all have a comparative narrow size distribution. The average sizes of the samples increase with the increasing of the reaction temperature, which are determined to be 6, 16, and 28 nm, respectively. The XRD patterns of the preparative NPs and the standard cards of the cubic phase NaYF4 and hexagonal-phase NaYF4 as control are shown in Fig. 1(g). As we see, the 6 nm NPs synthesized at 290 ÂºC was cubic phase, and the 16 and 28 nm NPs were both hexagonal phase. SEM images of PMMA PC template, TiO2 IOPCs, NaYF4 film, composite film, are shown in Fig. 2(aâ€“d), respectively. Fig. 2(a) shows that a long-range PMMA opal was formed, and the diameter of the PMMA spheres is 380 nm. From Fig. 2(b), it can be observed that the TiO2 IOPCs were long distance ordered structure, the center to center distance between the macropores is about 260 nm, which was about 32% smaller than the size of PMMA template because of the shrinkage of sphere di- ameters during calcination. Fig. 2(c) indicates that con- tinuous NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ films were developed on the glass substrate, composing of tight arranged NPs. Fig. 2(d) shows the top view of the composite film; it can be observed that after loading the NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ UCNPs, the interspaces of TiO2 IOPCs were occupied by NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ NPs. Fig. 2(d) shows the XRD patterns of composite film and TiO2 powder in contrast to the standard card (JPCDS: 84-1285) of anatase TiO2; it can be seen that the TiO2 powder exactly corresponding with the standard card. The XRD pattern of the composite film showed the diffraction peaks of cubic phase NaYF4 (marked with asterisks *) in addition to the TiO2 diffrac- tion peaks. However, some of peaks of TiO2 on the composite are too weak to display because of the strong diffraction of the glass substrate. Scheme 1 Formation process of composite film (a) and principle of FRET probe for mercury detection (b) Fig. 1 TEM photos of NaYF4 samples prepared under the temperatures of 290, 300, 320 ÂºC (aâ€“c), size contribution of the corre- sponding samples (dâ€“f) and XRD patterns of the NPs of different sizes with the standard cards of the cubic phase NaYF4 and hexagonal phase NaYF4 as control (g) XU Sai et al., Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of upconversion composite film and its application on â€¦ 463 Fig. 2 SEM images of PMMA PCs (a), TiO2 IOPCs (b), NaYF4 nanoparticles film deposited directly on the glass (c), NaYF4 nanoparticles/TiO2 IOPCs composite film (d), XRD pattern of composite film and TiO2 powder in con- trast of standard card of anatase TiO2 (e) 2.2 Effective UC luminescence enhancement of composite film In this article, the UC luminescence spectra of the NPs were recorded by the transmission method, and all the samples were measured and compared under the same measurement conditions. We first measured the size- dependence of the UC luminescence intensity of UCNPs thin films and UCNPs/TiO2 IOPC composite films under the excitation power density of 0.06 W/mm2. It is worth pointing out that the TiO2 IOPCs used in this work are all with the PSB of 650 nm, except for the PSB-dependence experiment. Fig. 3(a) shows the enhancement factor (EF) versus the nanoparticle size of NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ NPs on the composite film. It can be observed that with the in- creasing particle size of UCNPs, the EF decreased re- markably. To investigate the enhancement mechanism, we first recorded the transmittance spectra of NPs on the glass and the composite film to make sure the relative thick- ness of the NPs layer. The transmission of NPs layer on the glass and the composite film were referred as T1 and T2, respectively. In measurement of T1 and T2, the glass and the TiO2 layer served as reference. On the basis of Beer-Lambert Law, the relationship of the absorption and the transmission can be expressed as Ai=lg(1/Ti), i=1,2 (1) where the Ai and Ti represent the value of absorbance and Fig. 3 Size-dependence of the UCL enhancement factor (inset: a comparison of UCL spectra of the UCNPs film and the composite film with the 6 nm NaYF4:Yb,Er NPs) (a) and the UC population process of Yb3+/Er3+ couples (b) transmittance for the NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ film (i=1) and the composite film (i=2), respectively. The absorbance (Ai) and the thickness of the film (Di) are in linear relation- ship, so the relative thickness of NaYF4 in different sam- ples can be gained by the surveying of Ti. As is listed in Table 1, it can be seen that the relative thickness of the NaYF4 thin film and the NaYF4 layer on the NaYF4/TiO2 film hybrid gradually increases with the increasing of NaYF4 nanoparticle size. The thicknesses of the NaYF4 layer for the 28 nm NaYF4/TiO2 composite film are 3.66-fold and 1.26-fold compared to the 6 and 16 nm NaYF4/TiO2 composite films, respectively. When the ex- citation laser passes through the films, it should be ab- sorbed more by thinker film than by thinner film. Thus, the remanent being scattered by the IOPCs layer lessens, leading to the EF minishing with the increase of nanoparticle size. The comparison of UC spectra between the 6 nm- NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs formed on the glass and the same sized NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs deposited on the TiO2 IOPCs is shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a). Numbers of UC lines Table 1 Comparison of relative thickness of the NaYF4 layer with different sizes of NaYF4 UCNPs on the glass (D1) and TiO2 layer (D2) (Here the thickness of the 6 nm NaYF4 film was normalized) Thickness/nm 6 16 28 D1 (NaYF4 film) 1 3.29 4.31 D2 (composite film) 1.48 4.30 5.42 464 JOURNAL OF RARE EARTHS, Vol. 35, No. 5, May 2017 can be seen for both samples, the line in the blue region belongs to 2H9/2â†’4I15/2 (415 nm) of Er3+, the lines in the green region are derived from 2H11/2â†’4I15/2 (525 nm) and 4S3/2â†’4I15/2 (554 nm) transitions, and the line within the red region is attributed to the transitions of 4F9/2â†’ 4I15/2 (655 nm)[30]. The schematic diagram of UC populating and emission processes of NaYF4:Er3+,Yb3+ upon the ex- citation of the 980 nm laser is shown in Fig. 3(b). First, the Er3+ ion is excited from ground state to 4I11/2 via ET from Yb3+ to Er3+. Nonradiative relaxation of 4I11/2â†’ 4I13/2 populate the level 4I13/2 subsequently. In the second step, 980 nm laser excites the Er3+ ion from 4I11/2 to 4F7/2, then stepwise populates to 2H11/2 and 4S3/2. The radiative transitions from these excited states to ground state pro- duce two green lines 2H11/2â†’4I15/2 and 4S3/2â†’4I15/2. The populated level 4F9/2 of Er3+ ion mostly radiatively re- laxes to the the level of 4I15/2, which causes the emission 4F9/2â†’4I15/2. On the other hand, a portion of the electron on 4F9/2 level may be pumped to the 4G11/2 level via ET by the third photon, while the populated 4G11/2 level non- radiatively relaxes to the 2H9/2 state, then produce the blue emission. From Fig. 3(b), it also can be observed that the populations on 2H11/2, 4S3/2, and 4F9/2 originate from two-photon processes, however, the populations on 2H9/2 originate from a three-photon process. To further understand the UC enhancement mecha- nism, the UC spectra of 6 nm NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs/TiO2 IOPC composite film and the corresponding nanoparticle film under different excitation power density were re- corded. The dependence of the enhancement factors on diverse UC transitions and excitation power density is shown in Fig. 4. We can observe that the EF decreases with the increasing of excitation power density for all the three transitions. Similarly, when the excitation power density increases from 0.03 to 0.3 W/mm2, the entire emission intensity decreases from 32-fold to 11-fold. It also can be observed that for a certain power density, the EF of three-photon process is larger than the two-photon process. The above phenomenon shows that the UC im- provement might be related to the effect of IOPCs for Fig. 4 Power-dependence of the EF on different UC transitions for Er3+ excitation light. Besides, we measured the PSB-dependence of the UC spectra of 6-nm NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs/TiO2 IOPC com- posite film and the corresponding nanoparticle film un- der the same condition. The transmittance spectra of IOPCs with different PSB are shown in Fig. 5(a), as shown, the PSB locations distribute within the range of 500â€“1100 nm, which are covered with the excitation and emission lines of Er2+. The dependence of the entire EF from 350 to 750 nm that varies with the PSB location is shown in Fig. 5(b). It indicates that the EF stochastically changes in the range of 26 to 28, no matter whether the PSB location is covered within the excitation or emission location of Er2+. It is considered that as the depth of the PSB is not very deep, as shown in Fig. 5(a), the PSB modulation effect is not noteworthy. Based on the dis- cussion above, we deduced that the UC enhancement was mainly put down to stochastical diffraction of IOPCs multilayered structure to the excitation laser. 2.3 Detection of Hg2+ in serum with NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ NPs/TiO2 IOPCs composite film The enhanced solid-based UC sensor is desirable be- cause of its stability, convenience for biosensing. Gener- ally, for UC nanoparticles, owing large surface area and high UC emission intensity is contradictory. In this work, the composite film based on UC nanoparticles and TiO2 IOPCs we designed can enhance the UC intensity of small nanoparticles, which is promising to solve this problem. Fig. 5 Transmittance spectra of TiO2 IOPCs with different PSB (a) and the relationship of the UC EF and the PSB of TiO2 IOPCs (b) XU Sai et al., Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of upconversion composite film and its application on â€¦ 465 For mercury sensing, numerous fluorescent sensors based on QDs have been established because of their ad- vantages, such as high sensitivity, feasible and low price[31â€“33]. However, most methods reported for mercury detection were not in serum, but in aqueous solution. To our knowledge, serological proteins in serum can be ex- cited by UV and visible light, the emission light can se- riously influence the results of the detection based on QDs[34]. In this work, we chose a florescence resonant energy transfer (FRET) couple as florescence probe for the mercury sensing system, which employed PEI- UCNPs as energy donors and CdTe QDs with the excita- tion band located at 535 nm as energy acceptor. Under NIR excitation, the autofluorescence from serum and scattering light becomes negligible. Scheme 1(b) shows the principle for mercury detection, upon dropping QDs to UCNPs, the electrostatic interac- tion between the positively charged amino groups (Î¶ po- tential=+28.7 mV) of the PEI-UCNPs and negatively charged carboxyl groups (Î¶ potential=â€“15.8 mV) at- tached on the QDs makes the donor and acceptor close within a given proximity, excitation of NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs causes ET from Er3+ to QDs as the absorption spec- trum of the acceptor to overlap with the emission spec- trum of the donor (Fig. 6(a)), resulting in weakening the green emission of Er3+ and appearance of the emission of CdTe QDs. With adding the Hg2+ ions, Hg2+ ions bind to the surface of QDs due to the strong affinity of Hg2+ ions with the surface ligands of CdTe QDs, leading to fluo- rescence quenching of QDs. For biodetection application, the nanoparticles must be hydrophilic, we modified the 6-nm NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ NPs with PEI first. The TEM image of NPs after modification is shown in Fig. 7(a), which indicates the NPs has no ob- vious change on the morphology. HR-TEM image of the QDs is shown in Fig. 7(b), the average size of CdTe QDs was about 3.7 nm. To evaluate the sensitivity of the solid-based probe, the emission spectra of the QDs/ UCNPs/IOPC composite film by adding different amounts of Hg2+ in serum were measured. As shown in Fig. 6(b), upon adding Hg2+ ions, the emission intensity of QDs decreases continuously, while the emission in- tensity of Er3+ remains almost unchanged. As is shown in Fig. 6(c), the change of QDs fluorescence intensity relies on the concentration of Hg2+. The fluorescence quench- ing of QDs can be fitted by the Stern-Volmer equa- tion[35]: F0/F=1+K[Q] (2) where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of QDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. K represents Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] stands for the Hg2+ ion concentration. The calibration plot exhibits a fine linear relationship within the range of 0â€“2 Î¼mol/L (R=0.997), and the detection limit defined as 3Ïƒ/S was found to be 70.5 nmol/L, where Ïƒ represents the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the calibration plot. Compared to other reported fluorescent probe for mercury detection, the proposed method shows relatively good linear range and detection limit, as shown in Table 2[36â€“40]. Fig. 6 Emission spectrum of NaYF4:Yb,Er UCNPs; absorption and emission spectra of CdTe QDs (a), FRET spectra of bioassay ex- periments detected various concentrations of Hg2+ in serum (b), fluorescence intensity of FRET sensor as a function of Hg2+ concentration (c) and detection selectivity for Hg2+ (d) 466 JOURNAL OF RARE EARTHS, Vol. 35, No. 5, May 2017 Fig. 7 TEM image of PEI coated NaYF4:Yb,Er UCNPs (a) and HR-TEM image of CdTe QDs (b) Selectivity is a very important index to evaluate a bio- sensor. To determine the selectivity of the FRET biosen- sor for mercury detection, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cd2+ and Co2+ were investigated accompanied with Hg2+. From Fig. 6(d), it can be seen that no ions re- sulted in a fluorescence intensity change except for Hg2+. So, the sensor we developed exhibited a good selectivity for the target ion. Table 2 Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for mercury detection Probe Linear range/(nmol/L) LOD/(nmol/L) Ref. CdTe/CdS QDs 5â€“300 3.1 36 Graphene QDs 80â€“9000 10 37 C-dot 0â€“40000 200 38 Lanthanide chelates 10â€“2000 7.07 39 Organic polymer 0â€“30000 728 40 UC composite film 0â€“2000 70.5 This work 3 Conclusions In our study, we prepared NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ UCNPs/ TiO2 IOPCs composite film by self-assembly and solvent evaporation methods. 32-fold enhancement of UC lumi- nescence was examined in the composite films upon ex- citation of 980 nm laser. It also could be observed that the EF depended on the nanoparticle size, excited energy level of Er3+, power density of excitation laser, but inde- pendence of PSB of IOPCs. According to the above phenomena, the enhancement mechanism was put down to the stochastical diffraction of IOPCs multilayered structure to the excitation laser. In addition, on the basis of the enhancement effect of composite film, energy transfer between UCNPs and QDs, and the sensitive sensing of CdTe QDs on mercury, the UC composite film was used for sensing of Hg2+ in serum. The FRET solid sensor we developed had a fine linear relationship (R=0.997), low limit of detection (70.5 nmol/L), good selectivity, and could overcome the autofluorescence from serum excitation by visible light. Thus, the solid FRET biosensor may have great potential in mercury de- tection in biological field. References: [1] Chatteriee D K, Rufalhah A J, Zhang Y. Upconversion fluorescence imaging of cells and small animals using lanthanide doped nanocrystals. Biomaterials, 2008, 29: 937. [2] Liu Q, Sun Y, Yang T S, Feng W, Li C G, Li F Y. Sub-10 nm hexagonal lanthanide-doped NaLuF4 upconversion nanocrystals for sensitive bioimaging in vivo. J. Am. Chem. Soc., 2011, 133: 17122. [3] Wang F, Banerjee D, Liu Y S, Chen X Y, Liu X G. Up- conversion nanoparticles in biological labeling, imaging, and therapy. Analyst, 2010, 135: 1839. [4] Wang J, Wei T, Li X Y, Zhang B H, Wang J X, Huang C, Yuan Q. Near-infrared-light-mediated imaging of latent fingerprints based on molecular recognition. Angew. Chem. Int. Edit., 2014, 53: 1616. [5] Wang M, Mi C C, Wang W X, Liu C H, Wu Y F, Xu Z R, Mao C B, Xu S K. Immunolabeling and NIR-excited fluo- rescent imaging of HeLa cells by using NaYF4:Yb,Er up- conversion nanoparticles. ACS Nano, 2009, 3: 1580. [6] Zhou J, Liu Z, Li F Y. Upconversion nanophosphors for small-animal imaging. Chem. Soc. Rev., 2012, 41: 1323. [7] Zou W Q, Visser C, Maduro J A, Pshenichnikov M S, Hummelen J C. Broadband dye-sensitized upconversion of near-infrared light. Nat. Photonics, 2012, 6: 560. [8] Du P, Luo L H, Park H K, Yu J S. Citric-assisted sol-gel based Er3+/Yb3+-codoped Na0.5Gd0.5MoO4: A novel highly-efficient infrared-to-visible upconversion material for optical temperature sensors and optical heaters. Chem. Eng. J., 2016, 306: 840. [9] Du P, Luo L H, Park H K, Yu J S. Facile synthesis of Er3+/ Yb3+-codoped NaYF4 nanoparticles: a promising multi- functional upconverting luminescent material for versatile applications. RSC Adv., 2016, 6: 94539. [33] Zhang R Z, Chen W. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Facile synthesis and

item-info.ce:copyright: The Chinese Society of Rare Earths

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Foundation item: Project supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (11374044), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (3132016333, 3132017058), Doctor Start-up Funds of Liaoning Province (201601067), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M591420)

head.ce:title: Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of upconversion composite film and its application on mercury sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Transportation Equipment & Ocean Engineering College, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To achieve a stable, sensitive, and high-efficiency biological probe, a novel NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystals/TiO2 inverse opal composite film was designed by self-assembly and solvent evaporation methods. 32-fold enhanced upconversion (UC) emission was investigated under 980 nm excitation. According to size-dependency, excitation power density-dependency as well as photonic stop band (PSB)-dependency upconversion spectra, the enhancement mechanism of the composite film was put down to the stochastical diffraction of IOPCs multi-layered structure to the excitation laser. On the basis of the enhancement effect of the composite film, energy transfer between upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and quantum dots (QDs), and the sensitive sensing of CdTe QDs on mercury, the UC composite film was used for sensing of Hg2+ in serum. The solid sensor as a mercury detector owns lots of superiorities such as feasible operation, good linear relationship (R=0.997), low limit of detection (70.5 nmol/L) and thus may have broad prospects in the biosensing field.
Formation process of composite film (a) and principle of FRET probe for mercury detection (b)
